turns.content: I'm trying to write a match statement for my log parser but I keep getting an error about missing cases. Why does Rust force me to handle every possible variant even if some are unlikely?

Rust enforces exhaustive matches because the match operator is guaranteed to execute, so omitting a case would mean your program could panic at runtime with no warning during compile time. This prevents silent bugs where you think you covered all paths but actually forgot one branch that could be triggered in production. The compiler treats every missing arm as an error rather than a warning because incomplete logic in critical system code is unacceptable.
So I can't just ignore edge cases? What if there's some data format my parser never sees?

You don't have to ignore them, but you must explicitly account for them using the _ wildcard pattern which matches any value when no specific variant fits. For example, in a match on Option<T>, you'd handle Some(value) and None separately, then use _ to catch anything unexpected like invalid byte sequences or corrupted files. The _ wildcard acts as a safety net so your code explicitly states 'I know this case exists even if I don't implement logic for it yet,' rather than silently failing when that data appears.
